With certain exceptions, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question received after then will not get an answer, and in fact will just be deleted. If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says. 

The image? I've decided to trace, as far as I can, my own occult lineages in photos. Last week's photo was of Manly P. Hall, the third of Juliet Ashley's teachers; unlike the first two -- Edgar Cayce and Carl Jung -- Hall has a lineage that can be traced further back. When he was 18, enthusiastic, talented, and wet behind the ears, Hall arrived in California and spent several years studying with Augusta Foss Heindel, the head of the Rosicrucian Fellowship in Oceanside, CA. Born in Ohio, she moved to California as a young woman and became a skilled astrologer. In 1896 she met Max Heindel, then visiting from Europe, and they became close friends. After Heindel returned to California in 1909 he and Augusta renewed their friendship and eventually married; she taught him astrology, and he taught her the Rosicrucian teachings he had received in Europe. Max died in 1919, but Augusta kept the order thriving until her death in 1949, and her astrological lore as well as the Rosicrucian teachings found their way to Hall and, through him, to Juliet Ashley and John Gilbert.

Good Evening,

For anyone marking the anniversary of the September 11th attacks, my sympathy and condolences. To our host, thanks as always for hosting this space and answering these questions. Otherwise, it's fascinating to keep hearing about your occult lineage(s).

To Share: Today, a science fiction book: Anathem by Neal Stephenson. This book technically kinda-sorta violates our host's distaste for space as an overdone trope in sci-fi (explaining why it only kinda-sorta violates this would provide some pretty significant spoilers). Suffice it to say, "space travel" is an element in the story line, but is not the focus of what makes it science fiction. Perhaps most interesting to readers here, it proposes a Platonic view of the cosmos and deals rather seriously with both non-Christian monastic living and some pretty heavy philosophical/metaphysical sledding, all while providing a compelling narrative thread. I haven't re-read since becoming more religious, but it's long been my favorite science fiction work, and I hope it might intrigue some of y'all as well.

To Ask: I know that you (JMG) are not a natal astrologer and don't even play one on TV, but do the descriptions of Strength/Weakness by house you give in your Mundane Astrology write-ups on Subscribe Star apply to natal charts as well? Similarly, do rules like mutual reception apply to natal charts? I'm dipping my toes into astrology by interpreting my natal chart, and I'm trying to get a feeling for the weight to give to the different planets in it.

Thanks very much for your time and consideration,
Jeff

My current take is that Stephenson has a more realistic take on what's going on in the world than many of his Silicon Valley/Seattle tech-scene friends do, but that he still takes much of the religion of progress for granted.

Jeff

replies.title: Re: Coronation
replies.content:
The coronation of an English Sovereign is actually meant to be a Christian religious ritual, since that sovereign is not simply a secular official, but a religious one as well within the Church of England: (s)he is not just Head of State, but Head of (the State) Church as well.

There is also an earlier, much briefer, purely secular ritual, mentioned in the first paragraph of Section IV: The Oath": "her Majesty having already on Tuesday, the fourth day of November, 1952, in the presence of the two Houses of Parliament, made and signed the Declaration prescribed by Act of Parliament."

If the UK were ever to have a purely secular Head of State, it would most likely dispense with any coronation ritual whatever.

comments.title: Hand gestures
comments.content:
A few days ago I was reading about Runen-Yoga aka Runen-Gymnastik aka Runen-Asanas, essentially a form of yoga using the shapes of runes to channel energies. The author (Karl Spiesberger) discussed the hand gesture version (ie make the shape of the rune with the hand and fingers alone) and noted not only that this was related to Mudras, but also to hand gestures [Handgriffe] of the esoteric masonic tradition - citing for the Western tradition J.B. Kerning (Briefe zur Königlichen Kunst) and for the Turkish tradition von Sebottendorf (Die Praxis der alten türkischen Freimauerei). According to Spiesberger (my rough translation):

"The goal of the masonic gestures, as the letter mystics have probably applied them since time immemorial and still use them today, is to make the body 'sensitive to letters', to activate the etheric body, to awaken the chakras circulating in it; conditioned by a gradual 'transfer of molecules', the 'spiritualisation' of which the mystics speak, by calling forth an 'effect of rarefaction' which uncovers subtle powers of the nerves; the desired consequences of a 'word-magic condition of oscillation' produced by gesture and letter-thinking."

My question is whether you or any of your readers are able to provide details of any works in English which address these masonic hand gestures and their use?

Also, just a note for those readers who know runes and are following the Levi posts (and related to a comment I made on Ch 12 of Levi some while back about fitting runes to each chapter) that the same author in another work (Runenmagie: Handbuch der Runenkunde) lists the correspondences of tarot cards to the Armanen runes (amongst other correspondences, including astrological):

1. The SoP includes symbols for the Elements. For Fire and Water, the origin of the emblems (upward / downward pointing triangles) is obvious enough. However, other Golden Dawn textbooks state that that sphere of Light should be formulated inside the magician’s head, behind the eyebrows. Do you know the reason for that variation? replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Those are distinctive emblems of the tradition I received from my teacher John Gilbert, and as far as I know they're not found elsewhere. Based on what John said, his teacher Dr. Juliet Ashley created them on the basis of her own spiritual experience. The basic idea is that from the circle, which is spirit, one manifestation moves upwards -- this is air, which is also mind and consciousness -- and another moves downwards -- this is earth, which is also matter and embodiment. Fire and water then weave these two together.

2) It's a matter of choosing which energy center you want to activate. There are well over three dozen energy centers along the midline of the body from the soles of the feet to above the top of the head, and each one has different effects -- this is why it also makes a difference whether you place the Tiphareth center in the Middle Pillar at the heart or at the solar plexus. In terms of the two centers in/above the head, the one above the head connects to your higher self, while the one in your head activates your psychism; the former always struck me as the better choice, but your mileage may vary.

replies.content:
To answer a "why" question with divination I find it's crucial to understand what kind of "why?" is being asked for. "Why?" can mean "for what purpose?" or "due to what set of causes?" or "through what process?" or several other things. Something more complex than a three-card reading is probably going to be necessary: you might consider something like this:

1) this is the root cause of the event
2) this is the immediate cause of the event
3) this is the event itself
4) this is the immediate purpose of the event
5) this is the ultimate purpose of the event

You can do that with tarot, or any other oracle you like.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I have not yet learned to actually read tarot, so I can't help there, other than that as JMG said, a more-than-three card spread might be helpful.

As for Ogham, so far, most of my readings have been three card spreads of one kind or another (I find "Should I take this course of action?" with "Context, Action, Outcome" a very helpful one), but I have occasionally supplemented with follow up questions followed by a single card, something like "I'm stumped, what am I missing here?" Whatever kind of spread I do, I find that for me, anyway, Ogham cards seem to need to be read as a "gestalt" - sometimes the "future" card of a "past, present, future" spread gives me a clue about how to read the other two that doesn't feel exactly related to its temporal role. Also, a few other folks have commented, and it's seemed mostly true to me, that the Ogham will tend to "want" to say things about your inner/spiritual life, so it might work best on "why" questions in that area.

Lastly, there are some benefits that lot-casting methods, like non-card runestaves, can have for complex questions. Basically, the value is that you not only randomize which symbol ends up in which part of the "spread", you randomize whether or not symbols even show up in that part, and if so, how many of them. In case that's vague, here's an example: in his Runecaster's Handbook, Edred Thorsson recommends a "Nine Worlds Casting". Here, you have a cloth that is divided into the nine worlds of Norse mythology. Each world corresponds to different areas of interest/concern (for example, "Midgard" is about tangible manifestation and your ego, "Jotunheim" is about things outside of you that may challenge or confuse you, and so forth). You shake up the runestaves, close your eyes/look up, and drop them on the cloth. When you look down to interpret, you look at what runes landed in what area and start to piece together an interpretation. Any runestaves that land off the cloth are not relevant to the reading. Often, this method results in clusters of runestaves in a handful of worlds, with no runestaves in some others. You can use the distance between runestaves as a factor to consider too - close together means they are likely affecting each other or are somehow linked, far apart less so.

I find this method helpful for really big, thorny, open-ended questions like "what should I do next about my career" It would be way overkill for a daily reading, I think, and possibly so for a simpler "why" question.

A thought just occurred to me - if you put your ogham on sticks, you could work up a cloth with space for the seven elements and likely get some pretty good readings using the rune technique above, if you were already familiar with the DMH symbolism.

Hmmm, might have to experiment with this one.

Cheers,
Jeff

Egan's Law, "It all adds up to normality", is usually invoked in the context of reasons to believe in exotic materialist cosmology where it superficially feels like it's in conflict with intuitive folk physics. Like quantum superposition experiments failing to find a scale above which the many-worlds interpretation broke down, so that the seemingly more folk-physics-compatible Copenhagen interpretation would be able to "take over" above that scale. But Egan's Law would hold equally well in the "opposite" direction: whatever actually added up to it all is part of normality. And, after all, humanity's common history and enduring heritage are not things that one would want to suppress or falsify lightly.

To add to what JMG said, sometimes you have to admit that you're in something like a #P-hard problem, where you're trying to adjust many variables to all go along with each other in an impossibly large space of possibilities under a condition of Knightian uncertainty, and accept that your own background and the "random" events of the development process are going to affect the outcome. Which is to say, you're not even actually in the #P-hard problem, you're in a differently-constrained problem where the only things you actually can purposely do are things that you are capable of choosing to do, and there are multiple levels of ways to get confused about what that means.

Not that I'd know, myself, when to adopt which bits of "look for the real criteria for optimality hiding in the surrounding problem context, distill and crystallize them, and then follow them ruthlessly, avoiding putting any of yourself into the process", versus "throw whatever spaghetti you have at hand at whatever wall you happen to be standing near and see what sticks". replies.title: Re: Germanic Influences on Masonry
replies.content: You might be interested in "Nightside of the Runes" by Thomas Karlsson, he talks about this in the later part of the book.

replies.title: Re: Germanic Influences on Masonry
replies.content: Especially in early books like The Secret Teachings of All Ages, Hall was uncritical about claims made by 19th century occultists who made up a lot of things. The Masonic-style Norse mysteries are a good example. Outside the Rite of Memphis ritual and a few similar rituals, there wasn't much. replies.title: Re: Germanic Influences on Masonry
replies.content:
Not exactly what you're looking for, it sounds like, but there's a tiny bit of information on the Baldur myth as having to do with a ritual of initiation (perhaps a rite of passage for boys becoming men) in Odin: The One-Eyed God and the (Indo-) Germanic Mannerbund by Kris Kershaw. If I recall rightly, it might reference some carvings that have been interpreted as depicting Baldur's funeral, and are suspected of having been one of Snorri's sources for his telling of the myth. The main idea is that some of what Snorri tells in the Prose Edda might be a misinterpretation of what the carving was trying to show (which was an initiation ritual).

Also not exactly what you're looking for, but Thorsson has an initiatory course in book form called "The Nine Doors of Midgard", which if followed, is part of the process of getting admitted to the Rune Gild. From what little I know of Free Masonry, I suspect he applied at least his basic knowledge of lodge ritual forms to creating the course.

Hope some of this helps!
Jeff

comments.title: Reincarnation and Christianity
comments.content: JMG - I'm familiar with your perspective on reincarnation to the extent that it's covered in your FAQ.  Do you have thoughts on how following Christ (accepting as Lord and Savior) affects the reincarnation cycle?  Mainstream Christian theology (heaven and hell) seems hard to reconcile with reincarnation.  I'm not asking you to reconcile those, as it may be impossible and you may just say the mainstream Christian theology is wrong. But, I'm asking what the *real* impact of being a Christian would have in your understanding of reincarnation.  Thanks.

replies.title: Re: Reincarnation and Christianity
replies.content:
Plenty of Christians believe in reincarnation, though I know most of the major churches don't. As a polytheist, I have no trouble at all believing that Christ is a god and that he's perfectly capable, as gods are, of saving those who have a covenant with him and follow his commandments. Reincarnation doesn't change that. When you die, if you're not yet sufficiently purified from your bad karma (in Christian language, your sins) to rise to the next level of being (in Christian language, heaven), you would be reborn by God's grace in a setting where you would again be baptized and raised in the faith, and have another chance to work at it.

The Christians I know who believe in reincarnation have told me that from their perspective, Christ takes on the burden of original sin but not the consequences of your own individual actions; for those, to quote the gospels, you must pay the uttermost farthing; but since Christ is a merciful god, you have as many chances to do that as you need, and only if you systematically refuse every chance at betterment in life after life do you risk being entirely lost.

replies.title: Re: Reincarnation and Christianity
replies.content:
Yes, I agree with every point. I will further add that in the cosmology of Ft. Antonio Fortea, Earth is Purgatory, or rather it is the physical manifestation of the state of consciousness that is common with incarnated humans and the souls in Purgatory (which itself is a doctrine the Anonymous OP might or might not accept).

According to Fortea, the traditional teachings that place Heavens "up in the clouds" and Hells "down underground" do not match with the modern experience of exploring places not normally inhabitated by men. Instead, he places both Heaven and Hell in other planets (which he do not make any effort to identify, but just propose as a possibility). As preposterous as this sounds, it rings a bell if you are familiar with Dion Fortune's Cosmic Doctrine, which teaches that unrepentant, stubborn souls that refuse over and over again to learn the lessons that human existence poses will eventually be taken away from Earth by comets, and never be heard of again.

In his novel "The currents that bathe the Heavens", he presents the experience of this Purgatory from the point of view of (among others) two people: one baptized sinner (a female aide in a WW2 concentration camp, not a killer, but a cruel custodian) and a relatively decent hindu woman. The nazi one is suffering terribly for the memories of her actions on the flesh, though she receives encouragement from the angels and from other Christian souls. The hindu lady, having been left to figure things out for herself, is more anxious and perplexed than anything, but not particularly suffering. Being a Catholic priest in good standing with the Vatican, Fortea will never hint (or even consider) the possibility that devout pagans will receive spiritual help from their own gods in the afterlife; but I think he made a good job at describing what the afterlife must be like for an atheist.

In my personal view, one of the benefits of going to Christian Purgatory while in-between lives is that you can process your experiences and burn personal karma there. That should lead to being better prepared to fix your interpersonal/social karma once you are incarnated again.

replies.title: Re: Reincarnation and Christianity

As for Purgatory, that's always struck me as a very good description of the middle phase of the time between lives, when you're processing your experiences on the astral plane: to be more precise, that's Hell if you try to deny it and reject it, Purgatory if you suck it up and deal, and Heaven if you've dealt with your sins and so can rise to the contemplation of virtues and the vision of the divine.

comments.title: Five Tibetans/ Hindu dands and the use of symbolism  and mental imagery in energetic practices.
comments.content:
JMG,

Thanks as always for your generous sharing of your wisdom, knowledge and insights.

I've recently added the Five Tibetan Rites back into my morning routine. Usually right after LBRP and MP, or as a warm-up for other physical exercise. I have been liking the benefits so far! As I've been more and more curious about the lore surrounding this and other physical practices that have effects beyond the purely material plane , I recently got a copy of Re-Youth Yourself! The Hindu Secrets of Virility and Rejuvenation which -for those who may not know- is by Joseph Andrew Marcello 2016 expansion and annotation of a text by Emil Raux (published about a year prior to Kelder's book) that outlines "4 Hindu Dands" which are essentially identical to the "Five Tibetan Rites" minus the first rite (the clockwise spinning) and with a different sequence of operation. Raux's original text focuses mostly on using these as a way of increasing and protecting sexual potency in men, while Marcello points out that the popularity of the Five Rites argues strongly for their use for both sexes and as general tonic to the physical and energetic body.

My questions this week are prompted by an addition Marcello makes in his book, of a Taoist exercise to increase male sexual vigor called the Deer Exercise.

Included in the instructions are warnings about mixing this exercise with other modalities.

"Concentrate on what you are doing and feel the warmth grow. For all Taoist exercises, it is very important- indeed, it is necessary that you concentrate on the purpose of the physical motions, for doing so will enhance the results... Never try to use the mind to force the natural processes by imagining fires growing in the pubic area or any other area. This is dangerous."

and a little later..

"Do not try to force this with mental images."

His reasoning behind this warning, that the "energy" we are moving is "electrical energy" and that mental thoughts and images cannot move as fast as electrical energy strikes me as a little dubious and overly materialist, but the caution itself seems prudent...

as he goes on:

"The Deer Exercise is extremely safe- provided that it is not supplemented with techniques from other teachings. For show, various incompatible techniques are often thrown together to create spectacular techniques but the results are often disastrous."

In neither Kelder’s Tibetan Rites or Raux’s Hindu Dands do the instructions involve much in the way of energetic visualizations or symbolic content, though it’s acknowledged (and subjectivity experienced by many of us) to have effects on the other aspects of our person These practices seem to play along well with practices from multiple disciplines and occult or spiritual traditions.

You’ve talked about some of the risks that might be incurred from haphazard mix and matching and/or carelessly handling material without a qualified guru to guide one through (the deadly kundalini incidents that Manly P Hall recounts, and the illnesses and imbalances that can happen from mixing various Qi Gong exercises with Western Occult practices to name just a few).

So my questions:

1.) Is part of the danger due to the symbolic content of most of these disciplines and the danger that mixed or incompatible symbolism imprinted on the subtle bodies can have on the material body?

2.) Is there lower risk of contamination the other way around from practices where the focus is on the physical actions performed, even if it’s acknowledged that they may have potent effects on the etheric body/life force?

replies.title: Re: Five Tibetans/ Hindu dands and the use of symbolism  and mental imagery in energetic practices.
replies.content:
Delighted to hear this. I have a copy of Raux's booklet, as part of my small library of materials related to the Five Rites; it casts an interesting light on the prehistory of the Rites.

1) Yes, very much so.

2) Likewise yes. Some exercises are physically incompatible, but the difficulty is mostly energetic and etheric, so if you avoid that you're more likely to be fine.

comments.title: Maladaptive Daydreaming and the Astral
comments.content:
Good evening, JMG. I hope everything is well.
My question this week relates to a phenomenon known as maladaptive daydreaming. For anyone unfamiliar with the condition, it occurs when someone fantasises so much, or often that it actually affects their life negatively. There has been something of a buzz regarding the subject in psychological literature in recent years.
I struggle with maladaptive daydreaming, and have for many years now (although I didn't know it was a known phenomenon until less than a year ago.) I pace around my apartment imagining all sorts of (mostly) wonderful things happening to me for hours on end. Often, I will listen to music whilst doing this. These fantasies are very, very vivid. I can not only feel whatever relevant emotions are present, but occasionally temperatures, tastes, ect. Aside from quite literally spending entire days doing this, it has made me late for work, and interfered with my sleep many times now. While I've done quite a bit of thinking as to why I might do this on a psychological level, I'm curious about the magical component of this. I feel that as with many things, I won't be able to do much good unless I look at the immaterial causes/effects of this.
My questions are as follows:
1. I've read here that the imagination is linked to the astral. Does this mean that I somehow have "too much" astral energy stored up, and I need to release it somehow?
2. Should I/can I try to channel this energy into other creative pursuits? I'm thinking that if it is an abundance of creative energy, I'm probably fantasising because I want to create things, but don't as of yet have the will necessary to put in the time/effort.
3. Sometimes when I have these daydreams, they can interfere with the goal I am imagining. I don't just mean the obvious ones like not practicing an instrument because I'm busy thinking about how cool it would be to be really good at playing it. For example, if I'm spending a lot of time thinking about a girl I fancy, she'll often become less interested in me. Whereas if I think about her a more normal amount (ie; not maladaptively daydreaming about her,) this doesn't tend to happen as much. Is there any occult reason for this, or am I just undermining my own motivation to pursue goals by tricking my brain into thinking I have achieved them?

Thanks so much for reading this, I know it's probably a bit rambly, but I've never talked to anyone about this.

replies.title: Re: Maladaptive Daydreaming and the Astral
replies.content: I also struggle with this, just out of curiosity, what is your south node sign? Mine is Pisces and maladaptive daydreaming seems to me related in part to this

replies.title: Re: Maladaptive Daydreaming and the Astral
replies.content:
1) No, in fact you're probably doing too much on the astral and tiring yourself out there.

2) Very much so. I use a more adaptive version of the same thing to write fiction.

3) It's not just a matter of self-undermining. You have a certain amount of energy to create patterns; if you do it on the astral, you'll have less left over to do it on the material plane, and the material plane suffers accordingly.

If egregores count as occult powers, then there definitely are occult powers at play in this war. It's not only about material concerns, as most Western military analysts think; it's not even primarily about material advantages, economic power, and national security. Rather, the fundamental incompatibility of what it has meant to be a Russian and what it has meant to be a Ukrainian has lit the fire of this hostility and given it an endless supply of fuel.

Each nation sees itself as the only heir to the mantle of Kievan Rus' and Vladimir/Volodymyr the Great, and completely denies the legitimacy of the other side's claim to the same mantle. The irreconcilable differences between the two clashing egregores go back at least as far as the 1500s, if not earlier.

Initially the military action was provoked by Western (and to some extent, Ukrainian) aspirations to place nuclear weapons in Ukraine. But that just brought the clash of egregores to the forefront of the two nations' thoughts, and soon nuclear weapons became a secondary consideration.

I think Western--principally US--policy-makers bear the blame for the extremely grave dangers they have blundered into, taking us all there with them. They are delusional, and see war as simply another geopolitical technique for gaining more wealth and power, not as a life-or-death struggle for something much less material: the egregores of two nations. They have called up that which they do not know how to put down, to paraphrase Lovecraft.

If this ends in an all-out nuclear war between the West and Russia, a war to the last Russian and Euro-American standing, it will be these idiotic, arrogant policy-makers on our side who will be to blame.

I've also had the odd experience of finding a huge anomaly in Shakespeare that nobody else seems to have caught -- Tybalt in Romeo and Juliet, who "fences by arithmetic," is a very clear reference to the figure of the great swordsman Gerard Thibault, who was not publicly known in Britain until after Shakespeare died. So something odd is certainly involved! But it's not a major concern of mine; I'll see if a copy of the book shows up in the local library system.
